coredata.dc:title: An insight into electronic and optical properties of multilayer graphene quantum dots synthesized by hydrothermal approach
coredata.dc:creator: Jindal, Shikha
Giripunje, Sushama M.
coredata.dc:description: The carbonaceous small sized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) synthesized by hydrothermal route manifest the existence of band gap by cutting the graphene sheets into quantum dots (QDs). Consequently, the tuning of band gap from 0–3.2 eV occurs by decreasing the size of graphene owe to the quantum confinement effect. The small sized uniform particles corroborated from TEM analysis exhibits the excitation independent emission spectra in the UV excitation range. In addition, GQDs unveil the excitation dependent behavior by red shift in emission wavelength alongwith the decrease in intensity of photoluminescence upon excitation in the visible region. It happens due to the presence of varying size particles. The work function of GQDs was analysed by UPS spectroscopy and was found to be 4.48 eV. These electronic energy levels relies on the size and band gap of GQDs. The distinct optical and electronic properties leads to GQDs as an absorber, emissive and transport material in optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Hydrothermal process
Quantum dots
UPS
PL
Raman spectra

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are thankful towards CeNSE, Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore, funded by the Department of Electronics and Information Technology (DeitY), Govt. of India for carrying out the characterization facilities.We would also like to express our sincere thanks to Dr. N. P. Lalla, UGC-DAE-CSR, Indore for providing the TEM facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of GQDs and ICSD: 01-075-1621.
(a) SEM image of GQDs and (c) EDS spectra of GQDs.
(a) Low resolution TEM image of GQDs and inset is the SAED image of graphene; (b) High resolution TEM image of GQDs and (c) Particle size distribution of GQDs.

(a) UV–vis spectra of GQDs, (b) Plot of (αhν)2 vs hν, (c) PL emission spectra of GQDs, (d–f) Emission spectra of GQDs at excitation wavelength of 380, 420, 440 nm; respectively and (g) PLE spectra of GQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectra; and high-resolution spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s of GQDs.
(a) UPS spectra of GQDs and (b) Energy levels of GQDs.
EDS spectra of graphene quantum dots.
Surface elemental composition of graphene quantum dots determined by XPS.
Energy band information calculated from UPS and UV–vis absorption spectra.

head.ce:title: An insight into electronic and optical properties of multilayer graphene quantum dots synthesized by hydrothermal approach

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Visvesvaraya National Institute of Technology, Nagpur, Maharashtra, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbonaceous small sized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) synthesized by hydrothermal route manifest the existence of band gap by cutting the graphene sheets into quantum dots (QDs). Consequently, the tuning of band gap from 0–3.2 eV occurs by decreasing the size of graphene owe to the quantum confinement effect. The small sized uniform particles corroborated from TEM analysis exhibits the excitation independent emission spectra in the UV excitation range. In addition, GQDs unveil the excitation dependent behavior by red shift in emission wavelength alongwith the decrease in intensity of photoluminescence upon excitation in the visible region. It happens due to the presence of varying size particles. The work function of GQDs was analysed by UPS spectroscopy and was found to be 4.48 eV. These electronic energy levels relies on the size and band gap of GQDs. The distinct optical and electronic properties leads to GQDs as an absorber, emissive and transport material in optoelectronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: A two-dimensional graphene composed of sp2 hybridized C atoms exhibit zero band gap. The carbonaceous material graphene revealed the fundamental properties by reducing the graphite into single layer of graphene. A zero band gap does not show any auspicious optical properties [1]. However, it exhibits an infinite exciton Bohr radius by cutting the graphene sheets into particles smaller than this radius (typically below 20 nm) resulting into graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The graphene achieved the band gap in bilayer and multilayered graphene. Its band gap can be altered with surface functionalization by tailoring the shape, size, and edge states owing to its quantum confinement or edge effect [2,3]. Consequently, the band gap and non-toxicity of GQDs render it for electronics, optoelectronics and biological applications.

In recent times, graphene has attracted a lot of attention on account of their numerous properties and wide range of potential applications. Its extremely high room-temperature carrier mobility (≈20,000 cm2 V−1 s−1) makes graphene a promising candidate to replace the conventional semiconductor materials in the electric circuit. Graphene has been widely used in various fields such as solar cells, light emitting diodes, sensors, photo catalysis etc. [4–10]. Its higher carrier mobility makes the transportation of the charges swiftly to respective electrodes which reduces current losses via recombination and therefore improves the efficiency of a solar cell. Graphene has been studied as an electrode, transport material, absorption as well as emission layers in various electronic devices [11–14]. In addition, these QDs have been utilized in biological tagging and bio-sensing.

GQDs have been synthesized by various top-down and bottom-up approaches [15–19]. The bottom–up approaches include solution chemistry, carbonization [20,21] and top-down process includes hydrothermal cutting [22], electron beam lithography [23], electrochemical exfoliation [24] and surface passivating agents [25]. Bottom-up approach utilize complex precursors and large amount of waste material get produced and it also shows poor solubility. Instead, top-down route provides higher solubility. The numerous reports have been reported to investigate the GQDs. Permatasari et al. have reported the hydrothermal approach for the preparation GQDs by surface bonding with N and from the experiment they deduced the 5–6 layers of graphene and shows the excitation independent photoluminescence properties in the visible region [26]. Luo et al. have also investigated the hydrothermal route by changing the hydroxyl and epoxy groups to enhance the PL properties. They demonstrate the better optical properties by carboxyl groups present on the surface of QDs [27]. The optical properties depends on the size and composition of the material. Goharshadi et al. have demonstrated the 20 nm size GQDs synthesized by pyrolysis having the band gap 4.53 eV [28]. In this article, we have reported the facile top-down synthesis of GQDs by hydrothermal method and we obtained GQDs of 4–5 nm size with band gap of 3.2 eV. This band gap is quite appropriate for absorbance and emission processes. The structural, optical and vibrational properties of GQDs have been studied. Moreover, we have analyzed the UPS spectra to investigate the electronic energy levels. Hence, the work function, valence band and conduction band have been measured from UPS spectra to analyze the feasibility of material in various electronic devices such as solar cells and light emitting diodes. This investigation concludes that the GQDs can be utilized as a buffer layer as well as active layer material in semiconductor electronic devices.

Graphene platelets (15 μm, 2–10 nm thickness), sulphuric acid (H2SO4), nitric acid (HNO3) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were used as precursors. All the chemicals were of analytical grade and have been used without further purification.

The GQDs were prepared by using the hydrothermal process. In a typical experiment, 1.25 wt% graphene platelets powder was added in 30 ml conc. sulphuric acid and 10 ml of conc. nitric acid in the ratio of 3:1, then the reaction mixture was ultrasonicated for 12 h. This mixture was diluted with 250 ml of double distilled water followed by further ultrasonication. The sodium hydroxide was added to the reaction mixture to neutralize the pH value. Afterward, the mixture was transferred into the hydrothermal autoclave which was heated upto 200 °C and kept as it is overnight. After completion of the reaction, the autoclave was cooled down upto room temperature. The precipitate was washed with water several times. Finally, the GQDs were dried in the vacuum. The entire reaction process is shown in Fig. 1 .

X-ray diffraction spectra (XRD) was recorded by using Rigaku, Smart Lab X-R Diffractometer with the Cu Kα radiation. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained by Ultra 55 ZEISS a microscope and energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) was analyzed using X-MAX from Oxford instruments connected with SEM. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were analyzed by Technai G2 20 microscope at the operating voltage of 200 kV. The TEM sample preparation was done by dispersing the sample in methanol and coated on carbon coated copper grid. SAED pattern was collected to confirm the crystallinity of the sample. The optical absorption spectra was obtained with the help of Shimadzu, UV-3600 UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra was recorded by JASCO Spectrofluorometer FP-8200. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) spectra was recorded by Kratos Axis Ultra DLD. XPS spectra was collected by using the Al K-Alpha radiation (hʋ = 1486.6 eV) source at the accelerating voltage of 13 kV and UPS spectra was obtained using a He (I) laser source. Raman analysis was carried out using Horiba Jobin YVON Lab RAM HR spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm.

The structural analysis of GQDs was investigated by XRD. Fig. 2 depicts the XRD of GQDs and ICSD: 01-075-1621 of graphite material. The spectra show the diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 26.25° and 54.30° assigned to (002) and (004) crystal planes of hexagonal graphene, respectively. These results are well matched with the ICSD file no. 01-075-1621. Consequently, GQDs confirm the hexagonal structure of graphene with d spacing 0.339 nm. The thickness of graphene crystallite along the c axis was determined from (002) peak using Debye Scherrer’s formulaand was found to be 7.5 nm. Moreover, the peak of QDs is broadened due to the quantum confinement effect.

The surface morphology of nanoparticles was envisaged by SEM as shown in Fig. 3 . SEM image reveals the small and thin layers of graphene that confirms the cutting of the sheets into small sized QDs. Also, the EDS spectra was recorded to examine the elemental composition of the material. The spectra demonstrates the presence of C and O elements in the QDs. The spectra confirm the purity of the material as no other elements are present in the material. The percentage element composition is summarized in Table 1 . This analysis determines that the C is 92.20% along with the small range of impurity as O is 7.80%.

TEM analysis was done to calculate the particle size of the GQDs. Fig. 4 a, b depicts the low and high resolution TEM images of GQDs, respectively. TEM images display the spherical and uniform particles of graphene as QDs. The inset of Fig. 4b reveals the homogeneous particles. The particle size distribution of GQDs calculated from TEM image as shown in Fig. 4c. The statistical result demonstrates the average particle size of GQDs as 4.7 ± 0.164 nm. SAED pattern was recorded to recognize the crystallinity of material as shown in the inset of Fig. 4a. SAED pattern shows the uniform rings which was indexed as (002) and (004). These rings confirm the nanocrystalline nature of graphene. These results are consistent with the XRD results.

The optical absorption spectra of GQDs is demonstrated in Fig. 5 a. The spectra depict the broad absorption peak at wavelength 237 nm with band edge at 300 nm which can be attributed to the π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 hybridized C and n- π* transitions of CO bond, respectively [29]. This material reveals its absorption in near UV region. To investigate the band gap of GQDs, the graph of (αhν)2 versus hν was plotted as shown in Fig. 5b, by using the Tauc’s relation [30] as Eq. (1):
(1)
α h ν
n
= c
h ν -
E g

where hν is the photon energy, α is the absorption coefficient, c is constant relative to the material. The parameter n is associated with the different types of electronic transitions: n = 2, 1/2, 2/3 or 3 for the direct–allowed, indirect–allowed, direct–forbidden and indirect–forbidden transitions, respectively. Graphene is a direct band gap material. Thus, the value of n is chosen to be 2. The linear portion of (αhν)2 was extrapolated corresponding to hν equal to zero. Consequently, the band gap was found to be 3.2 eV. This increase in band gap is dependent on the size of the QDs.

The photoluminescence spectra of GQDs was recorded at different excitation wavelength of 340, 360, 380, 420 and 440 nm in UV and visible region as shown in Fig. 5c. The GQDs show the emission at wavelength 414 nm when it is excited at 340 nm and shows emission at 418 nm upon excitation at 360 nm. The spectra demonstrate the red shift in emission as the excitation wavelength varies from UV to visible region. Fig. 5d exhibits the emission peak at 407 nm along with the shoulder peak at 419 nm as the material was excited at 380 nm. The emission spectra shows nearly excitation independent PL when the material was excited at the UV wavelengths. The excitation-independence was explained that both the sizes and the surface states of sp2 C were uniform [31]. These excitation-independent peak positions and narrower PL spectra are because of the more homogeneous size distribution of the GQDs [32]. This excitation-independent PL behavior agrees with Kasha’s rule, i.e., emission occurs from the lowest lying electronically excited singlet state. Furthermore, the loss of vibrational excitation energy during the excitation-emission, which is the derivation of spectral red-shift (the so-called Stokes shift), may lead to the slight fluctuation of PL emission [33]. Whereas the emission spectra of GQDs were substantially excitation dependent upon the excitation in the visible region. Fig. 5e depicts the emission peak at 435 nm along with the broad peak in the range of 445–475 nm and 490–518 nm as the material was excited at 420 nm. These spectra also show broadened emission peaks at higher wavelengths. Fig. 5f reveals the emission peak at 459 nm accompanied by the shoulder peak at 466 nm as the material was excited at 440 nm. The broad and excitation-dependent emissions of GQDs were assigned to sp2 of different sizes and different emission sites of each sp2 clusters [31]. Generation of emissive traps, electronic conjugate structures, free zigzag sites and surface states due to varying sizes (TEM) of GQDs might be the reasons for this phenomenon [32]. As the excitation wavelength is changed from 300 to 400 nm, it is found that the PL peak shifts to longer wavelengths and its intensity decreases gradually [34].

The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum measured at the PL wavelength of 418 nm shows a peak at 360 nm while the PLE spectrum by monitoring 459 nm PL peak shows strong peak at 391 nm (Fig. 5g). The PLE spectra reflect that luminescence from the GQDs have different origins. The PLE spectra of GQDs with variable PL energy show a gradual change in the intensity indicating the existence of band tail by defect states (Fig. 3a). However, the prominent peaks in PLE spectra were not observed in the absorption spectrum, indicating that energy levels are closely spaced in GQDs causing broad absorption spectrum [35].

Fig. 6 displays the XPS spectra of GQDs. The survey spectra of GQDs (Fig. 6a) reveal the strong signal of C 1s as well as a weak signal of O 1s elements. The high resolution spectra of C 1s (Fig. 6b) was fitted in three distinct species. Thus, the peak at the binding energy of 284.1 eV is present due to CC of graphene. The second signal of C at binding energy of 284.5 eV confirms the presence of CC in graphene. Additionally, the third signal of C exhibits the bonding of C–O or CO at binding energy of 285.2 eV. Besides, the high resolution of O (Fig. 6c) displays the strong peak at binding energy of 531.6 eV with the apparent peak at 534.3 eV. These peaks demonstrate the oxygen atom is present in the lattice and on the surface of the material at the respective positions [36,37].

Moreover, the quantitative analysis was done to investigate the presence of constituent elements in the GQDs. Thus, the analysis shows the existence of C and O are 90% and 10% respectively. These results are relevant to the EDS results. This information is summarized in the Table 2 .

UPS spectra were carried out to determine the energy levels and work function of GQDs. The recorded spectrum is shown in Fig. 7 . From the spectra, valence band onset (Eonset) and secondary electron cut-off (Ecutoff) was estimated to be 1.9 eV and 16.74 eV, respectively. The work function was determined by the difference between the incident photon energy and secondary electron cut-off as represented in the Eq. (2):
(2)
ϕ = h ν -
E
c u t o f f

The valence band maxima (VBM) can be determined as per the Eq. (3) [38]:
(3)
V B M = h ν - (
E
c u t o f f
-
E
o n s e t
)

By using the incident energy, hν = 21.22 eV (He1 laser source); the calculated work function and valence band maxima was found to be 4.48 eV and 6.38 eV, respectively. However, the optical band gap was found to be 3.2 eV as calculated from the UV–vis absorbance spectra. Also, the conduction band minima (CBM) was determined to be 3.18 eV. The investigated data is summarized in Table 3 .

The vibrational properties of GQDs were analyzed by Raman spectra as shown in Fig. 8 . Raman spectra ascribed the three distinct peaks assigned to D vibrational band (1353 cm−1), G band (1584 cm−1) and 2D band (2705 cm−1). The D vibrational mode with A1g symmetry is present owed to the defects at the edges of the graphene. The existence of G vibrational mode is due to the first order scattering of the E2g mode attributed to the sp2 hybridized C atoms [39]. The two intense peaks G and 2D describes the number of graphene layers in the material. The second order D band results from the two phonon lattice vibrations. The broader 2D peak and the shift of G peak from 1581.6 cm−1 (graphite) to 1584 cm−1 confirms GQDs as multilayer material.

The hydrothermal method has been utilized to fabricate the GQDs. XRD spectra confirms the formation of hexagonal GQDs. TEM analysis reveals the spherical and uniform size particles with average particle size in the range of 4–5 nm. EDS and the quantitative XPS analysis shows the stoichiometry in atomic % of C and O elements. The multilayered GQDs was verified from Raman analysis. The existence of band gap and work function of GQDs as analyzed from UV–vis and UPS spectra confirms its potential application for optolelectronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful towards CeNSE, Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore, funded by the Department of Electronics and Information Technology (DeitY), Govt. of India for carrying out the characterization facilities.We would also like to express our sincere thanks to Dr. N. P. Lalla, UGC-DAE-CSR, Indore for providing the TEM facility.
